title: Fitting a picture rail
subtitle: Fix a continuous moulding at a consistent height around a room
excerpt: How to mark a level line, cut picture-rail moulding to length at internal and external corners, and fix it at a consistent height. Covers locating fixings into joists and noggins, mitring corners, and filling the back edge to the wall plaster.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: A picture rail is a horizontal timber moulding fixed 300 to 450 mm (12 to 18 in) below the ceiling.
Picture rail hooks
clip over the top edge and carry hanging wires, so no wall fixings are needed for pictures. The key step is establishing a true
datum height
around the room before a single fixing is made.
Step 1, establish the datum height

Decide the rail height from the floor, typically 2100 to 2300 mm (7 to 7 ft 6 in) in a standard ceiling room, or 300 mm below the ceiling coving. Mark this height at one point on the wall using a tape measure and pencil. From that single point, run the spirit level horizontally around the room, marking a continuous line at the top edge of the rail position. A water level or laser level speeds this step in a large room with out-of-plumb walls.
Step 2, locate fixing positions

Scan the wall with a cable-and-pipe detector along the rail line before marking any fixings. Find the joists or noggins by tapping: a solid thud indicates timber behind the plaster. On a lath-and-plaster wall, the rail can be fixed directly to any point where a lath crosses the line. On plasterboard over stud, fixings must hit a stud or noggin, or use cavity anchors for intermediate positions. Space fixings at no more than 600 mm (2 ft) intervals.
Step 3, cut the rail lengths and corners

Measure each wall length along the datum line. Cut internal corners as coped joints: mitre the first length at 45 degrees, then cope the second length to fit against the face of the first. Cut external corners as square mitres (45 degrees each side). On a standard single-rail room, measure each length long by 1 mm and spring it into position for a tight internal fit.
Step 4, fix and fill

Drill a 2 mm pilot hole through the rail at each fixing position, then fix to the wall with 50 mm oval or lost-head nails, or 50 mm No. 8 screws into timber. On masonry walls, drill and plug with a 6 mm wall plug and use a 50 mm No. 8 screw. Punch nail heads below the surface and fill. Caulk the back edge of the rail against the wall plaster before decorating. The top edge must be clean so hooks can slide.
Common mistakes
Marking from the ceiling rather than from the floor.
Ceilings in older houses are rarely level. Always measure from the floor and establish a true level datum.
Fixing at only one end of each length.
An unsecured rail flexes away from the wall under load. Fix at both ends and at no more than 600 mm (2 ft) intervals.
Cutting butt joints at internal corners.
A butt joint at an internal corner opens up as the timber moves. A coped joint remains tight through seasonal movement.
